elements.sent-2411-001: A simple, innovative, and efficient framework for vision pre-training with 3D Gaussian splitting (3D-GS) representation.
elements.sent-2411-002: Benefits from our effective pre-training diagram, downstream application for 3D perception tasks achieved great improvement, including 3D object detection, HD-map construction, and Occupancy prediction.. \end{center ].}
elements.sent-2411-003: Self-supervised learning has made substantial strides in image processing, while visual pre-training for autonomous driving is still in its infancy.
elements.sent-2411-004: Existing methods often focus on learning geometric scene information while neglecting texture or treating both aspects separately, hindering comprehensive scene understanding.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this context, we are excited to introduce GaussianPretrain, a novel pre-training paradigm that achieves a holistic understanding of the scene by uniformly integrating geometric and texture representations.
elements.sent-2411-006: Conceptualizing 3D Gaussian anchors as volumetric LiDAR points, our method learns a deepened understanding of scenes to enhance pre-training performance with detailed spatial structure and texture, achieving that 40.6\% faster than NeRF-based method UniPAD with 70\% GPU memory only.
elements.sent-2411-007: We demonstrate the effectiveness of GaussianPretrain across multiple 3D perception tasks, showing significant performance improvements, such as a 7.05\% increase in NDS for 3D object detection, boosts mAP by 1.9\% in HD map construction and {0.8\% improvement on Occupancy prediction.}
elements.sent-2411-008: These significant gains highlight GaussianPretrain’s theoretical innovation and strong practical potential, promoting visual pre-training development for autonomous driving.
elements.sent-2411-009: Source code will be available at {\textcolor{magenta {\url{https://github.com/Public-BOTs/GaussianPretrain.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: With the development of autonomous driving technology, vision-centered solutions have gradually attracted widespread attention.
elements.sent-2411-011: Many studies focus on extracting bird's-eye view (BEV) features from multi-view input images to address various downstream applications.
elements.sent-2411-012: While supervised methods dominate current research, their reliance on accurate ground truth labels presents a significant bottleneck due to the high cost and difficulty of acquisition.
elements.sent-2411-013: Conversely, the abundance and accessibility of unlabeled data offer a promising avenue for improving performance.
elements.sent-2411-014: However, effectively harnessing this unlabeled data remains a significant and ongoing challenge in the field.
elements.sent-2411-015: The core idea of self-supervised pre-training technology is to learn meaningful representations from abundant unlabeled data by leveraging carefully designed proxy tasks.
elements.sent-2411-016: There are several approaches have been developed to explore this topic, methods like UniScene~\cite{min2023uniscene and ViDAR~\cite{yang2024visual focus on predicting 3D occupancy or future LiDAR point clouds, effectively capturing geometric information but neglecting texture.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Conversely, Self-Occ~\cite{huang2024selfocc and UniPAD~\cite{yang2024unipad reconstruct 3D surfaces and RGB pixels from image-LiDAR pairs, thus capturing texture but relying solely on depth maps for geometric insights.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Although this aids in texture learning, these methods remain limited in extracting comprehensive geometric information.
elements.sent-2411-019: OccFeat~\cite{sirko2024occfeat , combines feature distillation with occupancy prediction to effectively capture both texture and geometry, but it introduces the complexity of an additional image foundation model and incurs significant pre-training costs.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Fortunately, 3D Gaussian Splatting (3D-GS), represented as point clouds, offers a powerful representation for scene reconstruction, encoding geometric and texture information through attributes like position, color, rotation, scaling, and opacity.
elements.sent-2411-021: Furthermore, compared to NeRF, 3D-GS achieves faster training convergence and requires less memory.
elements.sent-2411-022: These advantages address the key limitations in existing 3D pre-training techniques.
elements.sent-2411-023: Inspired by the success of 3D-GS in effective scene representation and MAE~\cite{he2022masked in 2D image self-supervised learning, we propose a novel pre-training approach GaussianPretrain, which combines 3D-GS with MAE method for pre-training tasks in 3D visual learning.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Our approach incorporates two key innovations: (i) LiDAR Depth Guidance Mask Generator.
elements.sent-2411-025: To enhance the efficiency of our approach, we only focus on learning the Gaussian information from a limited number of valid masked patches within the multi-view images.
elements.sent-2411-026: These patches are identified by an MAE strategy and further filtered to include only those with LiDAR depth supervision.. (ii).
elements.sent-2411-027: Ray-based 3D Gaussian anchor Guidance Strategy: For each LiDAR-projected pixel, a ray-casting operation into 3D space to sample the points within the voxel.
elements.sent-2411-028: We introduce a set of learnable Gaussian anchors of these points to guide the learning of Gaussian properties from the 3D voxel as volumetric LiDAR points and predict the relevant attributes (e.g., depth, opacity).
elements.sent-2411-029: This enables the model to simultaneously understand the geometry and texture information of the scene through 3D Gaussian Splatting.
elements.sent-2411-030: Finally, we reconstruct the RGB, depth, and occupancy attributes solely within the valid masked patches by decoding the Gaussian parameters.
elements.sent-2411-031: Compared to NeRF~\cite{mildenhall2021nerf based methods like UniPAD~\cite{yang2024unipad , our GaussianPretrain offers lower training time costs and memory consumption, while providing well understanding of the scene's representation.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: By combining these advantages, our GaussianPretrain enables to perform effective and efficient self-supervised pre-training.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{figs/cvpr-pretrain-compares-0.pdf} \caption{ Comparison of UVTR~\cite{li2022unifying} model performance on the nuScenes dataset with different pre-training framework: ImageNet, UniPAD~\cite{yang2024unipad}, and our GaussianPretrain.} \label{fig:top}
elements.sent-2411-033: To demonstrate the effectiveness and generalizability of GaussianPretrain, we conduct extensive experiments on various downstream vision tasks using the large-scale nuScenes\cite{caesar2020nuscenes dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-034: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:top , for 3D object detection, our pre-trained model significantly outperforms the ImageNet pre-trained UVTR baseline, achieving the gain in 8.99\% mAP and 7.05\% NDS.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Furthermore, our approach surpasses the previous state-of-the-art method, UniPAD~\cite{yang2024unipad , by 0.97\% mAP and 1.05\% NDS.}
elements.sent-2411-036: To further validate the generalizability, we evaluate our method on HD map construction and occupancy prediction tasks.
elements.sent-2411-037: GaussianPretrain surpasses the state-of-the-art MapTR~\cite{liao2022maptr method for HD map construction by 1.9\% in mAP.}
elements.sent-2411-038: For occupancy prediction, our approach achieves a {0.8\% improvement in mIoU over PanoOCC, setting a new SOTA performance.}
elements.sent-2411-040: To summarize, our main contributions are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-041: We introduce GaussianPretrain, a novel pre-training framework that integrates 3D Gaussian Splatting technology with a unified Gaussian representation.
elements.sent-2411-042: To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to leverage 3D-GS for pre-training diagram, representing a novel contribution to this field.
elements.sent-2411-043: We propose a simple yet effective framework by leveraging 3D Gaussian anchors as volumetric LiDAR points, combined with Ray-based guidance and MAE method.
elements.sent-2411-044: Providing an efficient solution for visual pre-training, significantly reduces time consumption and GPU memory costs without sacrificing detail.
elements.sent-2411-045: The comprehensive experimental results demonstrate the superiority and generalizability of GaussianPretrain, showcasing significant improvements across various 3D perception tasks, including 3D object detection, HD-map construction, and Occupancy prediction.
elements.sent-2411-046: These advancements set new performance standards and underscore GaussianPretrain's transformative potential in visual pre-training tasks for autonomous driving applications.
elements.sent-2411-047: Pre-training on 2D images has been highly successful, mainly using contrastive learning and masked signal modeling to capture semantic and texture information.
elements.sent-2411-048: However, pre-training for visual autonomous driving demands accurate geometric representation, introducing additional challenges.
elements.sent-2411-049: Several works are currently exploring this area.
elements.sent-2411-050: For instance, UniScene\cite{min2023uniscene and OccNet\cite{tong2023scene leverage occupancy prediction for pre-training, while ViDAR~\cite{yang2024visual predicts future LiDAR data from historical frame images.}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Although these methods are effective at capturing geometric information, but cannot learn detailed texture information.
elements.sent-2411-052: Conversely, methods like Self-OCC~\cite{huang2024selfocc , UniPAD~\cite{yang2024unipad , and MIM4D~\cite{zou2024mim4d use NeRF~\cite{mildenhall2021nerf to render RGB images and depth maps, learning texture but with limited geometric information.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: OccFeat~\cite{sirko2024occfeat employs knowledge distillation to transfer texture information from an image foundation model during occupancy prediction but incurs high pre-training costs.}
elements.sent-2411-054: In contrast, our work introduces 3D Gaussian Splatting for vision pre-training in autonomous driving, efficiently capturing both texture and geometry to address these limitations.
elements.sent-2411-055: Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF)~\cite{mildenhall2021nerf achieve impressive rendering quality by implicitly representing scenes of color and density, parameterized by Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs) combined with volume rendering techniques.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Follow-up works \cite{boss2021nerd,park2021nerfies,wang2024perf,xian2021space,irshad2023neo have successfully extended NeRF to various tasks, these methods still require extensive per-scene optimization, limiting their efficiency due to slow optimization and rendering speeds.}
elements.sent-2411-057: In contrast, 3D Gaussian Splatting~\cite{kerbl20233d explicitly represents scenes with anisotropic Gaussians, enabling real-time rendering via differentiable rasterization.}
elements.sent-2411-058: However, it tends to overfit specific scenes due to its reliance on scene-specific optimization.
elements.sent-2411-059: Recent approaches mitigate this by predicting Gaussian parameters in a feed-forward manner, eliminating the need for per-scene optimization.
elements.sent-2411-060: For example, GPS-Gaussian~\cite{zheng2024gps performs epipolar rectification and disparity estimation from image pairs, relying on stereo images and ground-truth depth maps.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Similarly, Spatter Image~\cite{szymanowicz2024splatter focuses on single-object 3D reconstruction from single views.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Both methods are often constrained by inefficiencies, limited to object reconstruction, and dependent on specific input formats such as image pairs or single views.
elements.sent-2411-063: In this paper, we extend 3D Gaussian Splatting into visual pre-training tasks, overcoming limitations related to the number of views and the necessity of depth maps by presetting fixed-position 3D Gaussian anchors in 3D space, marking a novel application of 3D-GS.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{figs/cvpr-model-1.pdf} \caption{The architecture of proposed GaussianPretrain. Given multi-view images, we first extract valid mask patches using the mask generator with the LiDAR Depth Guidance strategy. Subsequently, a set of learnable 3D Gaussian anchors is generated using ray-based guidance and conceptualized as volumetric LiDAR points. Finally, the reconstruction signals of RGB, Depth, and Occupancy are decoded based on the predicted Gaussian anchor parameters.} \label{fig:framework}
elements.sent-2411-064: Generally, scenes are represented by a set of Gaussians which are typically initialized from point clouds derived from reconstruction methods or LiDAR data.
elements.sent-2411-065: Each Gaussian is assigned with learnable attributes of orientation, color, position, scale, and opacity.
elements.sent-2411-066: During rendering, these 3D Gaussians are projected onto the 2D image plane using differentiable rasterization.
elements.sent-2411-067: For scene representation tasks, the Gaussian attributes are iteratively optimized by supervising the rendered output with ground truth images.
elements.sent-2411-068: For a Gaussian point in the 3D space, it is defined as \begin{equation}G(\mathbf{x}) = e^{-\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})}\end{equation} with $\mu$ and $\Sigma$ referring to the Gaussian mean center and the 3D covariance matrix.
elements.sent-2411-069: Projected into 2D, we have the 2D covariance matrix $\Sigma' = J W \Sigma W^T J^T$, where the viewing transformation is represented by $W$, and the Jacobian matrix $J$ corresponds to the linear approximation of the transformation.
elements.sent-2411-070: Finally, the pixel color is rendered from N ordered Gaussians with the blending equation \begin{equation}\label{reder_rgb} {C}(p) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} c_i \alpha_i \tau\end{equation} where $c_i$ is the Gaussian color represented with spherical harmonics and $\alpha_i$ is the opacity related influence of this Gaussian to the current pixel.
elements.sent-2411-071: The pipeline of our GaussianPretrain, a simple, innovative, and efficient framework for vision pre-training using 3D-GS representation, is illustrated in Figure.\ref{fig:framework .}
elements.sent-2411-072: Given multi-view images with valid masked patches, our goal is to reconstruct the signals, including RGB, Depth, and occupancy by decoding Gaussian parameters $\{ ({\mu _j, \alpha_j, {\Sigma _j, {c _j) \}_{j=1 ^{K}$ for each scene, where ${\mu _j$, $\alpha_j$, ${\Sigma _j$ and ${c _j$ are the 3D Gaussian's position, opacity, covariance, and color information, and $K$ denote the max number of Gaussian Anchors.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-073: In this section, we first detail the generation of valid masked patches under MAE method and Depth guidance in ~\Cref{sec:mask_generator .}
elements.sent-2411-074: Then, we introduce the initialization of 3D Gaussian anchors through Ray-based Guidance in ~\Cref{sec:anchor_generator .}
elements.sent-2411-075: Next, we review the process of converting multi-view images into a 3D voxel space in ~\Cref{sec:voxel_encoder .}
elements.sent-2411-076: Subsequently, we present the process of the Gaussian decoder part in \Cref{sec:gaussian_decoder .}
elements.sent-2411-077: Finally, the reconstruction task and GaussianPretrain' loss functions are present in ~\Cref{sec:losses .}
elements.sent-2411-078: Inspired by MAE~\cite{he2022masked , we apply random patch masking to multi-view images, denote as $M$.}
elements.sent-2411-079: Furthermore, sparse convolution is used to replace traditional convolutions in the image backbone, as implemented by SparK~\cite{tian2023designing which enhances the performance and generalization.}
elements.sent-2411-080: For computational efficiency, we only focus on learning Gaussian parameters from a limited set of valid masked patches.
elements.sent-2411-081: Additionally, we double-check the mask region by verifying the presence of LiDAR points within a certain depth range.
elements.sent-2411-082: As illustrated in \Cref{fig:motivation , if a set of points project into the masked patch $M_i$ in the images and their depth falls within the range of [a, b], the mask region will be marked as valid, $M'_i$.}
elements.sent-2411-083: The process is outlined as follows: \begin{equation}{M'}_{i=1}^n = valid, \text{if } Proj(\text{\textit{{Set}(pc)}}) \in \{[a, b], M\}\end{equation} where n represents the number of valid masked patches, with n $\leq$ m.
elements.sent-2411-084: This strategy ensures that our model concentrates on the foreground, avoiding unnecessary attention to irrelevant background elements like the sky.
elements.sent-2411-085: To enable the model to simultaneously understand the geometry and texture information of the scene, we introduce a series of learnable Gaussian anchors in 3D space.
elements.sent-2411-086: These anchors guide the learning of Gaussian properties derived from the 3D voxel grid, treated as volumetric LiDAR points.
elements.sent-2411-087: Consider the LiDAR-projected pixel denoted by $\mathbf{u =(u_1, u_2, 1)$ which corresponds to a ray $R$ that extends from the camera into 3D space.}
elements.sent-2411-088: Along this ray, we sample $D$ ray points $\{p_{j =\mathbf{u d_{j |j=1,...D,\ d_{j <d_{j+1}\}$, where $d_{j}$ is the corresponding depth along the ray.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-089: Each sampled ray point $p$ in the valid mask region $M'$ can be immediately unprojected to 3D space using the projection matrix summary as 3D Gaussian Anchors, $\mathcal{G _p^{M' (\cdot)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-090: This strategy not only eliminates the need for full image rendering, significantly reducing memory usage, but also enables simultaneous the RGB, depth, and occupancy reconstruction, a capability that has yet to be achieved by prior methods.
elements.sent-2411-091: Notably, our pre-training method is compatible with any type of view transformer.
elements.sent-2411-092: In our baseline model, UVTR~\cite{li2022unifying , we employ the lift-splat-shoot (LSS)\cite{philion2020lift and extend the channel dimension to incorporate a height dimension, producing 3D voxel features $V\in R^{C \times Z\times H\times W}$, where $C$, $H$, $W$, and $Z$ represent the channel number, dimensions along the $x$, $y$, and $z$ axes, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-093: Additionally, for each LiDAR-projected pixel, we perform a ray-casting operation to extract $N_t$ sampled target voxel where exists Gaussian Anchors $V_t$ from 3D voxel grid $V$.
elements.sent-2411-094: As shown in \Cref{fig:framework , by conceptualizing $\mathcal{G _p^{M' $ as 3D Gaussian anchors, this unified representation enables the efficient capture of high-quality, fine-grained details, providing a more comprehensive understanding of the scene.}}}
elements.sent-2411-095: Specially, each 3D Gaussian anchor is characterized by attributes $\mathcal{G =\{ x \in \mathbb{R ^{3 ,c\in \mathbb{R ^{3 ,r\in \mathbb{R ^{4 ,s\in \mathbb{R ^{3 ,\alpha \in \mathbb{R ^{1 \}$ and the proposed Gaussian maps $G$ are defined as: \begin{equation}G\left( x\right) =\{ \mathcal{M}_{c} (x),\mathcal{M}_{r} (x),\mathcal{M}_{s} (x),\mathcal{M}_{\alpha } (x)\}\end{equation} where $x$ is the position of a Gaussian anchor in 3D space, $\mathcal{M _{c}$, $\mathcal{M _{r}$, $\mathcal{M _{s}$, $\mathcal{M _{\alpha }$ represents Gaussian parameters maps of color, rotation, scaling and opacity, respectively.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-096: Due to the overlapping areas in the multi-view images, the pixel-by-pixel prediction of Gaussian parameters may lead to ambiguity due to overlapping splats.
elements.sent-2411-097: In contrast, we argue that predicting Gaussian parameters in a feed-forward manner directly from 3D voxel features is a better choice.
elements.sent-2411-098: Given voxel features $V$ and center coordinate $x$ , we employ trilinear interpolation to sample the corresponding feature $f(x)$ as follows: \begin{equation}f(x)=TriInter(V,x)\end{equation} The Gaussian parameter maps are generated by prediction heads, defined as $h=MLP(\cdot)$, which consist of multiple MLP layers.
elements.sent-2411-099: Each prediction head is specifically designed to regress a particular parameter based on the sampled feature $f(x)$.
elements.sent-2411-100: For the parameter of color and opacity, we employed the sigmoid function for a range of [0,1] as follows: \begin{equation}M_{c} (x)=Sigmoid(h_{c}(f(x)) M_{\alpha} (x)=Sigmoid(h_{\alpha}(f(x))\end{equation} where $h_{c}$, $h_{\alpha}$ denote the head of color and opacity.
elements.sent-2411-101: Before being used to formulate Gaussian representations, the rotation map should be normalized since it represents a quaternion to ensure unit magnitude while the scaling map needs activations to satisfy their range as follows: \begin{equation}M_{r} (x)=Norm(h_{r}(f(x)) M_{s} (x)=Softplus(h_{s}(f(x))\end{equation} where $h_{r}$, $h_{s}$ represents the rotation head and scale head.

GP is an abbreviation for GaussianPretrain.} \label{tab:camera_occ}
elements.sent-2411-102: To facilitate a better reconstruction of the masked region under the MAE strategy, we supervise the learning process with different reconstruction signals derived from the Gaussian representation.
elements.sent-2411-103: Specifically, the RGB, Depth, and Occupancy signals are decoded based on the predicted Gaussian anchor parameters within the valid mask patches.
elements.sent-2411-104: Since we do not need to reconstruct images of arbitrary perspectives, we directly predict fixed viewpoint RGB instead of using Spherical Harmonics {(SH) coefficients.}
elements.sent-2411-105: After predicting the parameters of the Gaussian anchor, we decode the color information with the \cref{reder_rgb to render the RGB values map of the image, denoted as $\hat{C}$, for each target reconstruct pixel.}
elements.sent-2411-106: Specifically, the $c_i$ value in the equation is replaced by the predicted RGB.
elements.sent-2411-107: Inspired by the depth implementation in NeRF-style volume rendering, we integrate the depth of each splat in a manner similar to RGB reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-108: We approximate the each pixel z-depth from the 3D-GS parameters.
elements.sent-2411-109: The process is as follows: \begin{equation}\hat{D} =\sum_{i=1}^n d_{i}\alpha_{i} \prod^{i-1}_{j=1} (1-\alpha_{j} )\end{equation} where n is the number of Gaussian anchors, $d_{i}$ is the $i^{th}$ Gaussian anchor $z$-depth coordinate in view space, enabling efficient depth rendering with minimal computational overhead.
elements.sent-2411-110: The opacity attribute of 3D-GS point is inherent to vision perception, particularly for occupancy prediction tasks.
elements.sent-2411-111: Unlike GaussianFormer\cite{huang2024gaussianformer , which uses opacity for semantic logits, we directly interpret opacity as an indicator of occupancy.}
elements.sent-2411-112: A Gaussian anchor with full opacity signifies the presence of an occupied location at $x$.
elements.sent-2411-113: Formally, for each target voxel, we take the maximum opacity value among Gaussian anchors within the voxel to represent the occupancy probability, denoted by $\hat{O}$.
elements.sent-2411-114: This direct mapping of opacity to occupancy provides a natural and effective way to leverage 3D Gaussian Splatting for occupancy prediction. \begin{equation}\hat{O} = \max_{j=1}^{k}{(\mathcal{M}^j_\alpha(x)) \mid x \in V_t}\end{equation} where $k$ is number of Gaussian anchors in a target voxel $V_t$.
elements.sent-2411-115: In summary, the overall pre-training loss function consists of color loss, depth loss and occupancy loss: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \linewidth{!}{$ \begin{gathered}L=\frac{\lambda_{RGB} }{N_t^p} \sum^{N_t^p}_{i=1} \left| C_{i}-\hat{C}_{i} \right| +\frac{\lambda_{Depth} }{N_t^p} \sum^{N_t^p}_{i=1} \left| D_{i}-\hat{D}_{i} \right| +\frac{\lambda_{Occupacy} }{N_t^v} \sum^{N_t^v}_{i=1} \left| O_{i}-\hat{O}_{i} \right| \end{gathered} $}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $C_{i}$, ${D _{i}$ are the ground-truth color, depth for each ray.}
elements.sent-2411-116: Each sequence is captured at 20Hz frequency with 20 seconds duration.
elements.sent-2411-117: Each sample contains RGB images from 6 cameras with 360$^{\circ}$ horizontal FOV and point cloud data from 32 beam LiDAR sensor, and five radars.
elements.sent-2411-118: For HD-map task, perception ranges are $[-15.0m, 15.0m]$ for the $X$-axis and $[-30.0m, 30.0m]$ for the $Y$-axis.
elements.sent-2411-119: We calculate the $\rm{AP _{\tau}$ under Chamfer distance with several thresholds ($\tau \in T, T=\{0.5, 1.0, 1.5\}$).}
elements.sent-2411-120: Occ3D-nuScenes~\cite{tian2023occ3d occupancy scope is defined as $-40m$ to $40m$ for X and Y-axis, and $-1m$ to $5.4m$ for the Z-axis.}
elements.sent-2411-121: The voxel size is $0.4m \times 0.4m \times 0.4m$ for the occupancy label.
elements.sent-2411-122: Occ3D-nuScenes benchmark calculates the mean Intersection over Union (mIoU) for 17 semantic categories (including `others').
elements.sent-2411-123: Besides, it also provides visibility masks for LiDAR and camera modality.
elements.sent-2411-124: Our code implementation is based on the MMDetection3D codebase, and all models were trained on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs.
elements.sent-2411-125: Unless otherwise specified, the input image resolution is set to 1600x900 by default.
elements.sent-2411-126: The scale factors for $\lambda_{RGB}$ and $\lambda_{Occ}$ are maintained at 10, while $\lambda_{Depth}$ is set to 1.
elements.sent-2411-127: During the pre-training phase, we apply a mask to the input images, with a mask size of 32 and a ratio of 0.3.
elements.sent-2411-128: LiDAR specific depth range for generating the valid mask is [0, 50].
elements.sent-2411-129: The model is pre-trained for 12 epochs using the AdamW optimizer, with an initial learning rate of 2e-4 and a weight decay of 0.01, without using CBGS [78] or any data augmentation strategies.
elements.sent-2411-130: In the ablation studies, unless explicitly stated, fine-tuning is conducted for 12 epochs on 50\% of the image data.
elements.subsection-2411-011: Main Results on Different Vision Task

elements.figure-2411-009: \fontsize{6pt}{8pt}\selectfont 0.85\textwidth{!} { \begin{tabular}{llcccccccccc} \multirow{2}{*}{Method} \multirow{2}{*}{Modality} \multicolumn{3}{c}{Distance: NDS(\%)} \multicolumn{2}{c}{Weather: NDS(\%)} \multicolumn{2}{c}{Lighting: NDS(\%)} \textless20{\em m} 20-30{\em m} \textgreater30{\em m} {\em Sunny} {\em Rainy} {\em Day} {\em Night} UVTR-C Camera 33.1 20.8 13.8 24.2 21.2 25.1 20.8 UniPAD-C Camera 39.0 27.3 15.8 29.8 26.1 31.0 25.4 GaussianPretrain Camera 39.1 28.9 16.9 30.8 27.1 32.1 26.5 \end{tabular} } \caption{Comparisons of different distances, weather, and lighting conditions on nuScenes {\em val} set. } \label{tab:condition}
elements.figure-2411-010: 0.9\columnwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{c|c|c|cc|c} RGB-Loss Depth-Loss OCC-Loss NDS$\uparrow$ mAP$\uparrow$ \textbf{{mIoU}} \ding{55} \ding{55} \ding{55} 25.23 23.00 15.1 $\checkmark$ 26.84 25.73 16.3 $\checkmark$ $\checkmark $ 29.20 26.54 17.2 \rowcolor[gray]{.92} $\checkmark$ $\checkmark $ $\checkmark $ 32.28 31.99 19.3 \end{tabular} } \caption{Ablation study on different supervised losses.} \label{tab:diff_loss}
elements.sent-2411-155: To validate the effectiveness of each reconstructed signal, we conducted experiments on UVTR and BEVFomer for 3D detection and occupancy tasks, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-156: The RGB loss guides the model in learning texture information of the scene from the reconstructed image, while the depth loss encourages the model to learn the geometric information on a 2D plane, although this alone is insufficient for capturing complete 3D geometry.
elements.sent-2411-157: In contrast, the occupancy loss supervises the model in learning comprehensive geometric information within 3D space.
elements.sent-2411-158: As shown in \cref{tab:diff_loss , we experiment on 1/2 subset for 12 epochs, each component contributes positively, with the best results achieved when all are used together.}
elements.sent-2411-159: Notably, thanks to the attribute of the opacity of Gaussian representation which inherently benefits occupancy prediction, yielding a significant improvement of 2.1\% in mIoU.
elements.sent-2411-160: These results underscore the effectiveness of Gaussian representation to the pre-training framework.
elements.figure-2411-011: 0.9\columnwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{c|c|c|c|c} Numbers latency(ms) Memory(MB) NDS$\uparrow$ mAP$\uparrow$ 256x100 16 502 31.17 30.94 512x100 17 608 31.70 31.55 \rowcolor[gray]{.92} 1024x100 19 788 32.28 31.99 2048x100 25 1170 32.42 32.08 \end{tabular} } \caption{Ablation study on the number of Gaussian anchors.} \label{tab:gaus_parms}

elements.figure-2411-012: 0.9\columnwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{c|c|l|c|c} Method Decoder Param Memory Latency UniPAD-C NeRF 0.46MB 1125MB 32ms GaussianPretrain 3D-GS 0.45MB 788MB 19ms \end{tabular} } \caption{Comparison the consumption with NeRF-based method.} \label{tab:consumption}
elements.sent-2411-164: NeRF-based methods often suffer from slow convergence and high GPU memory consumption.
elements.sent-2411-165: In contrast, our 3D-GS-based approach offers comparable rendering quality with significantly faster convergence and superior efficiency for free-view rendering.
elements.sent-2411-166: In the \cref{tab:consumption , we compare the efficiency and memory consumption of the decoder module between the NeRF-base UniPAD and ours.}
elements.sent-2411-167: Notably, GaussianPretrain obviously reduces memory usage by about 30\%, and decreases latency by approximately 40.6\%, while maintaining a similar parameter size.
elements.sent-2411-168: This highlights significant gains in resource efficiency with the GaussianPretrain approach.
elements.figure-2411-013: \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{figs/cvpr-compares-9.pdf} \caption{ Effect of GaussianPretrain on Fine-tuning. By reducing annotations from the full training set to a 1/4 subset). } \label{fig:training-stage}
elements.sent-2411-169: We demonstrate the effectiveness of GaussianPretrain in reducing the reliance on annotations by fine-tuning UVTR~\cite{li2022unifying , ranging from the full dataset to a 1/4 subset.}
elements.sent-2411-170: As depicted in ~\cref{fig:training-stage , our approach surpasses the baseline under full supervision by 5.5\% mAP, with only half of the supervised samples, i.e., 32.0\% mAP vs. 26.5\% mAP.}
elements.sent-2411-171: This result indicates that GaussianPretrain can effectively leverage unlabeled data to compensate for reduced supervision, leading to improved performance even with fewer annotations.
elements.sent-2411-172: We report the performance with different distances, weather conditions, and light situations in the \cref{tab:condition , which benefits from our effective pre-training on UVTR-C, GaussianPretrain achieves superior robustness and overall the best performance.}
elements.sent-2411-173: In this work, we introduce 3D Gaussian Splatting technology into vision pre-training task for the first time.
elements.sent-2411-174: Our GaussianPretrain demonstrates remarkable effectiveness and robustness, achieving significant improvements across various 3D perception tasks, including 3D object detection, HD map reconstruction, and occupancy prediction, with efficiency and lower memory consumption.. Limitation.
elements.sent-2411-175: There still exist certain limitations in the current framework.
elements.sent-2411-176: Notably, it does not explicitly incorporate temporal or multi-modality information, both of which are crucial for many autonomous driving applications.
elements.sent-2411-177: In future work, we plan to extend GaussianPretrain to leverage this information and further enhance its performance.. { \bibliographystyle{ieeenat_fullname.}}
